claim_text: Information-weighted SSIM (IWSSIM10) weights SSIM by assigning higher importance to regions of the image based on their information content.

sub_claims.text: zhouwang2011 defines an Information-Content-Weighted SSIM measure (IW-SSIM) that combines information-content weighting with the multiscale SSIM (MS-SSIM) algorithm.

evidence.section: III. APPLICATIONS / B. Information Content Weighted MultiScale SSIM
evidence.snippet: By combining information content weighting with multiscale SSIM, we define an information content weighted SSIM measure (IW-SSIM). Let [w] be the information content weight computed at the [j-th] spatial location in the [k-th] scale using (28), the [k-th] scale IW-SSIM measure is defined as (45) ... The final overall IW-SSIM measure is then computed as (47) using the same set of scale weights ['s as in MS-SSIM.

We propose a multiscale information content weighting approach based upon a GSM model of natural images [39]. We show that this novel weighting method leads to significant and consistent performance improvement of both PSNR- and SSIM-based IQA algorithms ... Our extensive tests with six publicly-available independent image databases show that the proposed IW-SSIM algorithm achieves the best overall performance.
We validate the proposed IW-PSNR and IW-SSIM measures and compare them with 13 other algorithms.
the best overall performance is achieved by combining information content weighting with multiscale structural similarity measures.

sub_claims.nuance: The source uses the canonical name 'IW-SSIM'; the manuscript's local tag 'IWSSIM10' is a manuscript-side label (almost certainly the citation/reference number in the manuscript) and is not a separate variant in zhouwang2011.

sub_claims.text: In zhouwang2011's IW-SSIM, the per-location SSIM contributions are weighted by an information-content weight derived from a GSM/mutual-information model of natural images, so that image regions with higher local information content receive higher (perceptually more important) weight in the pooled quality score.

evidence.snippet: Based upon the approach given in [34], at each location, the information of the original and distorted images perceived by the visual system can be computed by the mutual information ... Here we move one step further to estimate the total perceptual information content from both images. ... This results in a total information content weight measure given by (12).

This process results in an information content weight map for each scale. An example of the 'Einstein' image is given in Fig. 4. By visually inspecting the reference and distorted images, we observe that the information content is distributed unevenly over space. For example, compared with the background, the eye regions and some sharp edge areas in the images are perceptually more informative. As expected, these observations are well represented by the information content maps, where brighter indicates more information content and, thus, higher visual importance in IQA.

evidence.section: III. APPLICATIONS / B. Information Content Weighted MultiScale SSIM
evidence.snippet: Let [w_{i,j}] be the information content weight computed at the [j-th] spatial location in the [k-th] scale using (28), the [k-th] scale IW-SSIM measure is defined as (45) ... [a per-location IW-weighted average of the local SSIM map at each scale, then pooled across scales].

We believe that our results support the general principle underlying our approach, i.e., the optimal weight for pooling should be directly proportional to local information content measured in units of bit.
the optimal perceptual weights for pooling should be proportional to local information content, which can be estimated in units of bit using advanced statistical models of natural images.

sub_claims.nuance: Mechanism is explicit and matches the manuscript's framing: per-location information-content weights (computed from a GSM mutual-information model on a Laplacian-pyramid decomposition) replace the uniform mean used in standard SSIM/MS-SSIM, so high-information regions (e.g., 'eye regions and sharp edges') contribute more to the pooled IW-SSIM score.

phrasings_tried: IW-SSIM / IWSSIM / IW SSIM / IW-SSIM10 (definition probe)
information content weighting / information-content weight (mechanism probe)
weight by information content / weighted by ... information (paraphrase probe)
regions / region of interest / importance / perceptually important / visual importance (region-importance probe)
combining information content weighting with multiscale SSIM (definition phrasing)
higher weight ... higher information / brighter indicates more information content (paraphrase probe)
GSM / mutual information / perceptual information content (underlying-model probe)

sections/C. Interpretation of VIF Based Upon Information Content Weighting.txt
figures/ (not consulted; textual evidence is sufficient and explicit for the definitional claim)

attestation.indirect_attribution_check: zhouwang2011 is the primary source for IW-SSIM: it defines the measure (Eqs. 45-47 in §III-B) and proposes the underlying multiscale information-content weighting framework (§II). It builds on but does not delegate the IW-SSIM definition to other works (it cites SSIM [5], MS-SSIM [42], VIF/[34], and GSM [39] as components, but the IW-weighted SSIM construction itself is original to this paper).
attestation.out_of_context_check: Manuscript usage ('IW-SSIM weights SSIM by assigning higher importance to regions of the image based on their information content') matches the source's framing exactly: §II derives an information-content weight map (with the example noting 'eye regions and sharp edge areas' receive 'higher visual importance'), and §III-B substitutes that weight map into the multiscale SSIM pooling so high-information regions contribute more to the final score. No context mismatch.
